title: Trailing SL of the Nasdaq Algo
author: Some_Fly_4552

comments.author: dogazine4570
comments.body:
Hard to give specifics without seeing your logic, but for Nasdaq algos a fixed trailing SL often gets chopped up because of the volatility spikes around opens and news.

A few things that helped me:

- Use ATR-based trailing instead of fixed ticks/points. For NQ, something like 1.5–2.5x ATR on your trading timeframe adapts better to regime changes.
- Consider structure-based trailing (last swing high/low) once price moves >1R. It avoids getting wicked out by noise.
- Split exits: take partial at a defined RR (e.g., 1.5R–2R), then trail the rest more loosely. That smooths equity curves.
- Disable or widen trailing during high-impact events (CPI, FOMC). NQ loves stop hunts during those windows.

Also check if your trailing logic is evaluated on every tick vs. bar close — that alone can massively change backtest vs live behavior.

If you can share timeframe and entry logic (breakout, mean reversion, etc.), people can give more targeted feedback.

replies.author: Some_Fly_4552
replies.body:
Thanks for the feedback definitely, I will look at it😊. So it is on the 3M timeframe and I can give you like a short description of the actual bot. My bot trades NAS100 on the 3-minute chart using a smart trend-following strategy. It combines moving averages, volatility filters, and higher-timeframe confirmation to catch trades in line with the bigger market move.

Each trade is split into 4 partial positions, so profits can be taken step by step while still leaving room for bigger runners. On top of that, session and trading-day filters help avoid low-quality setups.
